prompt:
**Exercise 1 — Unstructured data models (1 PT).** Consider the ER diagram tracking Disney's digital products: `Company Section` –*Produce* (N:M)– `Digital Content` {`Series`, `Movie` {`Animated Movie`}} –*Describe* (1:N)– `Script` –*Include* (1:N)– `Line`; `Character` –*Play* (1:1 Character / 1:N Actor)– `Actor` {`Voice Actor`} –*Perform* (1:N Actor / 1:1 Line)– `Line`; `Script` –*Located* (1:N Script / 1:1 Location)– `Location`.

Which DB technology would be best to store each entity/relationship? Fill a table with columns ENTITIES/REL., DB TYPE, MOTIVATION (you may not need every row).
solution:
| ENTITIES / REL. | DB TYPE | MOTIVATION |
|---|---|---|
| `Character`, `Actor`, `Voice Actor`, `Play`, `Perform` | **Graph DB (Neo4j)** | The interesting part of this sub-model is the *web of relationships* (which actor plays which character, who performs which line) rather than the attributes themselves; a graph makes traversals like "actors who play ≥3 characters" or "voice actors of a given role" natural and index-free-adjacency fast. |
| `Company Section`, `Digital Content`, `Series`, `Movie`, `Animated Movie`, `Script` | **Document DB (MongoDB)** | This is a classic *aggregate/hierarchy*: a digital content has a variable, type-dependent shape (series vs. movie vs. animated movie) and owns a set of scripts. A schema-flexible document store lets each content type carry only its own fields and embed its scripts, avoiding many-table joins. |
| `Location` | **Search engine (Elasticsearch)** | `Description` and `Furniture` are free text that must support full-text/relevance search (e.g. "stadium", "basketball"), which only an inverted-index search engine handles efficiently with scoring. |
| `Line` (as a fast, simple lookup/queue of short strings) | **Key-value store (Redis)** | Lines are small, immutable, sequentially accessed strings (e.g. "first three lines of a script"); a key-value/list store gives O(1) push/range access without the overhead of a richer model. |

No single technology fits the whole diagram well: the graph part needs relationship traversal, the content/script part needs flexible nested documents, `Location` needs text search, and `Line` benefits from a lightweight KV/list structure — this is the classic polyglot-persistence argument for unstructured/NoSQL systems.

prompt: **Exercise 2 — Neo4j (5 PT).** Consider the entities `Character`, `Actor`, `Voice Actor`, and `Line` from the ER model (Exercise 1). Sketch a graph model/example describing the nodes, main attributes, and edges (either an example instance graph or a graph with node/relationship types). (1 PT)
solution:
**Type graph:**

```
(:Character {name, nickname, age})
      -[:PLAYED_BY]->
(:Actor {personalId, name, surname, age, birthDate, careerDescription})
      -[:PERFORMS {recordingDate}]->
(:Line {id, sequenceNumber, text})
```

`Voice Actor` is modeled as an **extra label** on the same node rather than a separate node type (ISA/subtype in the property-graph world), carrying its own extra properties:

```
(:Actor:VoiceActor {personalId, name, surname, age, birthDate, careerDescription, voiceTone, voiceStrength})
```

**Example instance:**

```
(c1:Character {name:"Morpheus", nickname:"Neo's mentor", age: 45})
(a1:Actor:VoiceActor {personalId:"A001", name:"Mark", surname:"Smith", voiceTone:"deep", voiceStrength: 4})
(l1:Line {id:"L001", sequenceNumber: 1, text:"There is no spoon."})

(c1)-[:PLAYED_BY]->(a1)
(a1)-[:PERFORMS {recordingDate: date("2024-01-10")}]->(l1)
```

The `PLAYED_BY` edge captures the `Play` relationship (each `Character` played by exactly one `Actor`, one `Actor` may play many `Character`s), and `PERFORMS` captures `Perform` (each `Line` performed by exactly one `Actor`, storing the `Recording Date` attribute on the edge).

prompt: **Exercise 2 (cont.).** Write a Cypher query to collect the total number of lines that were performed by actors named "Mark" who performed at least 100 lines with at least three characters. (2 PT)
solution:
Using the graph model above (`(:Character)-[:PLAYED_BY]->(:Actor)-[:PERFORMS]->(:Line)`):

```javascript
MATCH (a:Actor {name: "Mark"})-[:PLAYED_BY]-(c:Character)
MATCH (a)-[:PERFORMS]->(l:Line)
WITH a, COUNT(DISTINCT c) AS numCharacters, COUNT(DISTINCT l) AS numLines
WHERE numCharacters >= 3 AND numLines >= 100
RETURN SUM(numLines) AS totalLines
```

The first `MATCH` finds, for each actor named "Mark", the distinct characters he plays; the second finds the distinct lines he performs. `WITH` aggregates per-actor counts and the `WHERE` clause keeps only actors satisfying both thresholds (≥3 characters, ≥100 lines). Since several actors could be named "Mark", the final `SUM` adds up the qualifying actors' line counts to get the requested total.

prompt: **Exercise 2 (cont.).** Write a Cypher query to return the collection of voice actors who played at least 15 characters nicknamed "Jack" and who acted at least once as "Morpheus", performing at least 1000 lines in the latter role. (2 PT)
solution:
The base ER model only records *who performs a line* (`Perform`), not *which character was being played* while that line was spoken. To answer "lines performed **in the role of Morpheus**" we need that extra piece of information; a natural, minimal extension is to attach the character name to the `PERFORMS` edge (e.g. `PERFORMS {recordingDate, asCharacter}`) — this is a modeling assumption made explicit here, not part of the original diagram.

```javascript
MATCH (va:Actor:VoiceActor)-[:PLAYED_BY]-(jack:Character {nickname: "Jack"})
WITH va, COUNT(DISTINCT jack) AS jackCount
WHERE jackCount >= 15
MATCH (va)-[:PLAYED_BY]-(morpheus:Character {name: "Morpheus"})
MATCH (va)-[p:PERFORMS {asCharacter: "Morpheus"}]->(l:Line)
WITH va, jackCount, COUNT(DISTINCT l) AS morpheusLines
WHERE morpheusLines >= 1000
RETURN DISTINCT va
```

The query first filters voice actors who play ≥15 characters nicknamed "Jack", then, among those, requires at least one `Morpheus` role and ≥1000 lines performed while `asCharacter = "Morpheus"`, finally returning the qualifying voice-actor nodes.

prompt: **Exercise 3 — MongoDB (7 PT).** Consider the entities `Company Section`, `Digital Content`, `Series`, `Movie`, `Animated Movie` and `Script` (and all its child entities). How many collections would you define within a documental database? How would you implement the relations between the concepts? Provide a simple documental representation. (1 PT)
solution:
**Two collections** are enough:

- `companySections` — one document per `Company Section`.
- `digitalContents` — one document per `Digital Content`, using a discriminator field `type` (`"series"` / `"movie"` / `"animatedMovie"`) to hold the type-specific attributes of the ISA hierarchy (`Series`, `Movie`, `Animated Movie` are all just different shapes of the same collection — "single table/collection inheritance"). Since a `Script` only ever describes **one** `Digital Content` (1:N `Describe`, N side has no further sharing), scripts are **embedded** as a sub-array inside their digital content document — no separate `scripts` collection is needed. The `Produce` relationship between `Company Section` and `Digital Content` is N:M, so it is best implemented by **referencing**: each digital content stores the array of `companySectionIds` that produced it (or, symmetrically, each company section could store an array of content ids — pick the direction queried most often, here it's "per company section" so we keep it in `digitalContents`).

```json
// companySections
{
  "_id": "908686",
  "name": "Digital Artist & Co",
  "description": "...",
  "numberOfEmployees": 120
}

// digitalContents
{
  "_id": "cinderella-1950",
  "title": "Cinderella",
  "releaseDate": "1950-02-15",
  "duration": 74,
  "productionCost": 2900000,
  "minimumAge": 0,
  "type": "animatedMovie",
  "sequenceNumber": 12,
  "nominatedForAward": true,
  "companySectionIds": ["908686", "AN-01"],
  "scripts": [
    { "id": "S001", "length": 120, "duration": 105, "title": "Cinderella" }
  ]
}
```

Embedding scripts favors read locality (a content and its scripts are almost always fetched together); referencing company sections avoids duplicating section data across every content they co-produce.

prompt: **Exercise 3 (cont.).** Write a query to return the number of digital content generated by each company section, keeping only the ones who produced at least 20 of them. (1.5 PT)
solution:
```javascript
db.digitalContents.aggregate([
  { $unwind: "$companySectionIds" },
  { $group: { _id: "$companySectionIds", count: { $sum: 1 } } },
  { $match: { count: { $gte: 20 } } }
])
```

`$unwind` turns each content's `companySectionIds` array into one row per (content, section) pair, `$group` counts contents per section, and the final `$match` keeps only sections with 20 or more.

prompt: **Exercise 3 (cont.).** Write a query to count the number of series produced by a company section named "Digital Artist & Co" with ID "908686" and for which at least one script includes the word "Pier" in its title. (2 PT)

**Exercise 3 (cont.).** Write a query to return the total count of the scripts of animated movies produced by at least one company section named "Animation", for which at least one script has a duration greater than 100 minutes and a title equal to "Cinderella". (2.5 PT)
solution:
Filtering by company-section **name** ("Animation") requires a `$lookup` into `companySections`, since `digitalContents` only stores section ids:

```javascript
db.digitalContents.aggregate([
  { $match: { type: "animatedMovie" } },
  { $lookup: {
      from: "companySections",
      localField: "companySectionIds",
      foreignField: "_id",
      as: "sections"
  } },
  { $match: { "sections.name": "Animation" } },
  { $unwind: "$scripts" },
  { $match: { "scripts.duration": { $gt: 100 }, "scripts.title": "Cinderella" } },
  { $count: "totalScripts" }
])
```

We keep only `animatedMovie` documents produced by at least one section named "Animation" (checked via `$lookup` + `$match` on the joined array), then unwind the embedded `scripts` array and keep the scripts matching the duration/title condition, finally `$count`ing them.

prompt: **Exercise 4 — Elasticsearch (4 PT).** Consider the `Location` entity (`ID`, `Name`, `Description`, `Furniture` (text), `Indoor` (Yes/No)). Provide the complete mapping of the index (field name, field type, structure of the mapping, etc.) (1 PT)

**Exercise 4 (cont.).** Write a query to return the list of Locations whose description includes the words "Stadium" and "Basketball", assigning a higher score to those including the word "Planet" in their furniture and "Space" in their description. (1.5 PT)
**Exercise 4 (cont.).** Write a query to return the count of indoor and outdoor locations. (1.5 PT)
**Exercise 5 — Redis (3 PT).** Consider the `Line` entity. Write the Redis code to create a new list of lines involving the lines "Hey you", "Watch out", and "Hello there". (1 PT)
solution:
```javascript
RPUSH lines "Hey you" "Watch out" "Hello there"
```

`RPUSH` creates the key `lines` (a Redis **List**) if it doesn't exist and appends the three strings in order at the tail, so the list ends up as `["Hey you", "Watch out", "Hello there"]`.

prompt: **Exercise 5 (cont.).** Write the Redis operation to collect the first three lines in the list. (1 PT)
solution:
```javascript
LRANGE lines 0 2
```

`LRANGE` returns elements of a List by index range; Redis list indices are 0-based and the end index is inclusive, so `0 2` returns the first three elements (`"Hey you"`, `"Watch out"`, `"Hello there"`).

prompt: **Exercise 5 (cont.).** Write the Redis operations to create a new key-value pair named "Line #0001" with the value "A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away" and check its lifespan. (1 PT)
solution:
```javascript
SET "Line #0001" "A long time ago in a galaxy far, far away"
TTL "Line #0001"
```

`SET` creates the plain string key-value pair. `TTL` reports the key's remaining time to live in seconds; since `SET` was called without an expiration option (e.g. `EX seconds`), the key is persistent and `TTL` returns **-1** (no expiry set). If an expiry were desired one would instead write `SET "Line #0001" "..." EX 3600` and `TTL` would then return the remaining seconds (e.g. `3600` right after creation).
